Home › Departure from Thermal Equilibrium

Departure from Thermal Equilibrium

Departure from Thermal Equilibrium is the third Sakharov Condition required to generate a matter-antimatter asymmetry. In a system at perfect thermal equilibrium, the rates of any reaction and its inverse are identical (Detailed Balance), meaning any local excess of matter would be instantly erased. For the universe to choose matter over antimatter, it must have undergone a rapid "Snap" transition where the expansion of space occurred faster than the subatomic particles could re-equilibrate.

1. The Principle of Detailed Balance

In equilibrium, the probability distribution of states is determined solely by the Maxwell-Boltzmann or Fermi-Dirac distributions. If a process exists that violates baryon number (\( B \)), its inverse process will occur at exactly the same rate in equilibrium. This ensures that the Net Baryon Number remains zero. To "freeze in" an asymmetry, the system must be pushed away from this maximum-entropy state.

2. Cosmic Expansion as the Driver

The primary way the universe leaves equilibrium is through Adiabatic Cooling driven by expansion. If the Expansion Rate (\( H \)) is greater than the Interaction Rate (\( \Gamma \)) of a specific particle species, that species "Freezes Out." It can no longer find partners to annihilate or transform, effectively locking its current abundance—including any asymmetry—into the structure of the cosmos.

3. First-Order Phase Transitions

In Electroweak Baryogenesis, the departure from equilibrium occurs during a First-Order Phase Transition. As the Higgs field acquires a non-zero value, bubbles of the "New Vacuum" expand and collide. The moving bubble walls provide a violent, non-equilibrium environment where CP-Violating interactions with quarks can generate a baryon excess that is then swept into the bubble interior and protected from further change.

4. Out-of-Equilibrium Decay

In models of GUT Baryogenesis or Leptogenesis, the asymmetry is generated by the decay of massive, unstable particles (like heavy Higgs bosons or right-handed neutrinos). If these particles decay when the temperature of the universe is lower than their mass (\( T \lt M \)), they are effectively "Over-abundant" relative to equilibrium. Their subsequent decays, if they violate B and CP, naturally produce a net surplus of matter.

5. The Baryon-to-Photon Ratio (eta)

The magnitude of the final asymmetry is quantified by \( \eta \approx 6 imes 10^{-10} \). This tiny number represents the "Efficiency" of the non-equilibrium process. For every billion pairs that annihilated back into photons in the early universe, one single matter particle was saved because it was created in a region of Thermal Disequilibrium. This leftover residue is what constitutes the 4.9% of the universe that we call ordinary matter.

6. Boltzmann Equations for Baryogenesis

To calculate the asymmetry, physicists solve a set of coupled Boltzmann Equations: \( \frac{dn_B}{dt} + 3Hn_B = ext{Source} - ext{Washout} \). The "Washout" term represents processes that try to return the system to equilibrium. Successful baryogenesis requires that the Source (driven by B and CP violation) exceeds the Washout (minimized by the departure from equilibrium), providing a rigorous dynamical map of the birth of matter.

Key Theoretical Identities

Interact with identities to explore their semantic depth.

  • Equilibrium Eraser [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ H > \Gamma \quad ( ext{Freeze-out condition}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Equilibrium Eraser: The rule that ensures every matter-producing reaction is perfectly canceled by its inverse.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Expansion Victory [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ \frac{dn_B}{dt} + 3Hn_B = \int C[f] d\Pi \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Expansion Victory: The moment when space grows so fast that particles can no longer interact to maintain symmetry.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Phase Boundary [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ n_{eq} \propto e^{-M/T} \ll n_{actual} \quad ( ext{Out-of-equilibrium}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Phase Boundary: The non-equilibrium front where the Higgs field changes, potentially forging the matter excess.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Asymmetry Decay [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ \Delta B \propto \int (\Gamma_{fwd} - \Gamma_{rev}) dt \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Asymmetry Decay: The competing processes that try to destroy any newly generated baryon number.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.
